old_claim.note: Bills of Exchange Clearing provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The boards resolved debts, written confirmations, agreements, and other mercantile obligations, so contract-law concepts are the direct legal substrate; bills of exchange are only one possible dispute subject.

source_shape.source_locator: Introduction: Sachs describes a 1293 St. Ives fair-court debt dispute involving written confirmation and deed validity, and frames the court's business as commercial plaints arising out of the fair.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source documents fair-court adjudication of mercantile debt and document-validity disputes.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports contract_law as the prerequisite because the board scope depends on adjudicating commercial obligations and remedies. Bills-of-exchange clearing is a transaction/payment practice and can be a dispute subject, but it is not required for merchant arbitration boards to exist.

ontology_before: Merchant Arbitration Boards depended on bills_of_exchange_clearing, laundering one financial instrument and clearing practice into the prerequisite boundary for broader commercial adjudication.
ontology_after: Merchant Arbitration Boards depends on contract_law as the broader obligation/remedy substrate, while bills of exchange remain outside scope as a possible dispute domain.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: merchant_arbitration_boards now depends on contract_law and no longer depends on bills_of_exchange_clearing.
would_reject_if: The node is narrowed specifically to boards for bill-of-exchange clearing disputes.
A source shows medieval merchant arbitration boards required clearing systems rather than contract/debt obligations.
The contract_law node is narrowed away from commercial agreements, obligations, debt, sale, agency, partnership, or remedies.
short_reason: Commercial adjudication needs contract obligations; bills clearing is only one possible subject matter.
